structures.name: Three-Act Structure
structures.description: The classic Western narrative framework: setup, confrontation, resolution. Works for almost any story length and genre.

beats.name: Setup
beats.description: Establish ordinary world, introduce protagonist, present inciting incident, propel into Act II

beats.name: Confrontation
beats.description: Rising action, midpoint reversal, escalating obstacles, all-is-lost moment

beats.name: Resolution
beats.description: Climax, dramatic question answered, new status quo established

structures.name: Hero's Journey
structures.description: Joseph Campbell's monomyth adapted by Christopher Vogler. A cyclical transformation structure: departure, initiation, return.

beats.name: Ordinary World
beats.description: The hero's normal life before the adventure

beats.name: Call to Adventure
beats.description: The challenge or quest is presented

beats.name: Refusal of the Call
beats.description: Fear, hesitation, or obligation holds the hero back

beats.name: Meeting the Mentor
beats.description: A guide appears with wisdom or tools

beats.name: Crossing the Threshold
beats.description: The hero commits to the journey

beats.name: Tests, Allies, Enemies
beats.description: The hero learns the rules of the special world

beats.name: Approach to the Inmost Cave
beats.description: Preparation for the major ordeal

beats.name: The Ordeal
beats.description: The hero faces their greatest fear or foe

beats.name: Reward
beats.description: The hero claims what they came for

beats.name: The Road Back
beats.description: The hero must return, often chased or pressured

beats.name: Resurrection
beats.description: A final test where the hero applies everything learned

beats.name: Return with the Elixir
beats.description: The hero returns transformed, bearing gifts for the ordinary world

structures.name: Save the Cat
structures.description: Blake Snyder's beat sheet with precise percentage targets. Originally for screenplays, now widely used in novel plotting.

beats.name: Opening Image
beats.description: Snapshot of the protagonist's starting state

beats.name: Theme Stated
beats.description: Someone hints at the story's thematic question

beats.name: Set-Up
beats.description: Introduce world, characters, stakes

beats.name: Catalyst
beats.description: The inciting incident

beats.name: Debate
beats.description: The protagonist wrestles with whether to act

beats.name: Break into Two
beats.description: The protagonist commits to the journey

beats.name: B Story
beats.description: Subplot (often a relationship) that carries the theme

beats.name: Fun and Games
beats.description: The promise of the premise delivered

beats.name: Midpoint
beats.description: Stakes raised; false victory or false defeat

beats.name: Bad Guys Close In
beats.description: External pressures tighten, internal flaws surface

beats.name: All Is Lost
beats.description: The lowest point; something or someone is lost

beats.name: Dark Night of the Soul
beats.description: The protagonist processes the loss

beats.name: Break into Three
beats.description: The protagonist finds a new way forward

beats.name: Finale
beats.description: The protagonist confronts the conflict with new understanding

beats.name: Final Image
beats.description: Snapshot of the protagonist's transformed state

structures.name: Seven-Point Story Structure
structures.description: Dan Wells' streamlined framework. Works well for plotters who want flexibility within structure. Can be expanded or compressed to any length.

beats.name: Hook
beats.description: The protagonist's starting state -- the opposite of the ending state

beats.name: Plot Turn 1
beats.description: The inciting incident. The world changes.

beats.name: Pinch 1
beats.description: Pressure applied. The antagonist or opposition forces the protagonist to act.

beats.name: Midpoint
beats.description: The protagonist moves from reaction to action. They decide to push forward.

beats.name: Pinch 2
beats.description: More pressure. The situation looks impossible. The protagonist is squeezed.

beats.name: Plot Turn 2
beats.description: The protagonist learns what they need to succeed. The final piece falls into place.

beats.name: Resolution
beats.description: The protagonist applies what they've learned. The story's question is answered.

structures.name: Kishotenketsu
structures.description: A four-act structure common in East Asian storytelling. There is no central conflict or antagonist. Instead, the story develops through contrast and revelation. Conflict is optional.

beats.name: Ki (Introduction)
beats.description: Establish the world and characters. Set the baseline.

beats.name: Sho (Development)
beats.description: Expand on the introduction. Deepen and complicate without conflict.

beats.name: Ten (Twist)
beats.description: An unexpected element is introduced -- a surprise that reframes everything.

beats.name: Ketsu (Conclusion)
beats.description: The twist is reconciled with the introduction. A new understanding emerges.

structures.name: Fichtean Curve
structures.description: Rising-action-driven structure with no initial equilibrium. The story starts with conflict and escalates continuously through crises toward a climax and resolution.
bestFor: stories that start in medias res

beats.name: Rising Action
beats.description: A series of escalating crises. No setup period -- start with the first conflict.

beats.name: Climax
beats.description: The tension peaks. The protagonist faces their greatest challenge.

beats.name: Falling Action
beats.description: The aftermath of the climax. Consequences play out.

beats.name: Resolution
beats.description: New normal established. The protagonist has changed.

structures.name: Snowflake Method
structures.description: Randy Ingermanson's iterative expansion approach. Start with a one-sentence summary, expand to a paragraph, expand each sentence to a paragraph, and so on. A plotting method rather than a structure, but it produces three-act-shaped stories.

steps.description: Write a one-sentence summary of your story
Expand the sentence into a paragraph: setup, three disasters, ending
Write a one-page summary for each major character
Expand each sentence from step 2 into its own paragraph
Write a one-page synopsis from each major character's POV
Expand the four-paragraph plot into a four-page synopsis
Expand character summaries into full character charts
Create a scene list from the synopsis
Write a narrative description of each scene
